bio.bio_blurb: Seth Hart's son who settled in New York

cross_connections.display_label: made Britannia lamps as Bradley made brass ones in Meriden

narrative_blocks.header: He made his life in New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Of Seth's children, Lucius left Connecticut for New York City, where he married Hannah Robinson. He made his life there until his death in 1871, far from the Rocky Hill of his childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Half the pewter firm Boardman & Hart
narrative_blocks.body: For two decades Hart was the New York partner in Boardman & Hart, one of the country's leading makers of Britannia and pewter ware. The firm's wares sold across the country from its Manhattan showrooms.

narrative_blocks.header: His pewter survives in museum collections
narrative_blocks.body: Tea sets, urns, and beakers stamped Boardman & Hart and Lucius Hart & Co. survive today in collections of early American pewter. Examples are held by museums documenting the nineteenth-century metalware trade.

narrative_blocks.header: A tailor's granddaughter who married a commodore
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah, granddaughter of a Rocky Hill tailor, married the banker and yachtsman E.C. Benedict in 1859. She presided over Indian Harbor, his estate on the Greenwich shore.

narrative_blocks.header: A lost son broke her health for good
narrative_blocks.body: When her only son Frederick was killed in a 1901 automobile accident, Sarah's health collapsed. She remained under a physician's care until her own death seven years later.

notable.notable_blurb: Gilded Age banker, yachtsman, and friend of Cleveland

cross_connections.display_label: had his secret 1893 cancer surgery aboard the Oneida

narrative_blocks.header: A president's secret surgery on his yacht
narrative_blocks.body: In 1893 Benedict lent his yacht Oneida to his friend President Grover Cleveland for a secret operation to remove a cancerous growth from the president's jaw.

narrative_blocks.header: The dish eggs Benedict may bear his name
narrative_blocks.body: One enduring origin story credits the Commodore with the first eggs Benedict, a recipe said to have surfaced decades later in a letter to a newspaper food editor.

narrative_blocks.header: Robert Peary named a Greenland fjord for him
narrative_blocks.body: A restless yachtsman who loved strange waters, Benedict had Benedict Fjord in Greenland named in his honor by the explorer Robert Peary.

bio.bio_blurb: Benedict heir who married Flagler's daughter

cross_connections.display_label: co-founded Standard Oil and was father of his wife Jennie

narrative_blocks.header: His wife died at sea on the Oneida
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick married Jennie, daughter of Standard Oil's Henry Flagler. She died at sea aboard the yacht Oneida in 1889, and their only child died the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed when his car overturned in a storm
narrative_blocks.body: A widower by his forties, Frederick was killed near West Point in 1901 when his automobile rolled in a storm. A guest riding with him was gravely hurt.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; President of Union Theological Seminary

narrative_blocks.header: She married the architect of the public library
narrative_blocks.body: Helen's husband, Thomas Hastings, designed the New York Public Library and the Frick mansion. Their 1900 wedding drew New York's wealthiest, with Stanford White decorating the church.

bio.bio_blurb: Benedict daughter who married aviator Clifford Harmon
New York lawyer at Lord, Day & Lord

cross_connections.display_label: had Beers draw up the guardianship papers for his children

narrative_blocks.header: A senior partner at Lord, Day & Lord
narrative_blocks.body: Beers practiced law in New York for decades as a senior member of the old firm Lord, Day & Lord and sat on corporate boards.

narrative_blocks.header: He handled legal affairs for the Roosevelt family
narrative_blocks.body: Beers prepared the papers naming Edith Roosevelt guardian of two of Theodore Roosevelt's children, Kermit and Ethel.

cross_connections.display_label: ran the great railroads and was father of his wife Mary

narrative_blocks.header: Robert died young
narrative_blocks.body: Robert died in 1867, in his first year.

narrative_blocks.header: Alice died young
narrative_blocks.body: Alice died in 1869, aged about one.

career.notes: Continued his father’s firm with Charles E. Huntington after 1871.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost all four of his children young
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius Hart Jr. and his wife buried all four of their children, none living to carry on the family name. The losses left him without direct heirs at his death in New York in 1898.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried on his father's metalware firm
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius Hart Jr. learned the silverplating trade in his father's New York house, and on the elder Hart's death in 1871 he and Charles E. Huntington kept the business alive. They renamed it the Lucius Hart Manufacturing Company, continuing the Britannia and metalware line the firm was known for.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm's wares survive in museum collections
narrative_blocks.body: The Hart workshops turned out Britannia, pewter, and silverplated goods for the American household trade through the middle of the century. Pieces bearing the Hart marks are preserved today in collections such as the Winterthur Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Carrie died young
narrative_blocks.body: Carrie died in 1869, aged about two.

narrative_blocks.header: Henry died young
narrative_blocks.body: Henry died in 1882, aged about eleven.

narrative_blocks.header: Florence died young
narrative_blocks.body: Florence died in 1873, aged about two.

narrative_blocks.header: Lucius died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lucius died in 1881, aged about two.
